attack_summary: An attacker used a compromised Linux host (UbuntuComputer, agent rnxwtm at 192.168.1.3) to run an ICMP flood (hping3) targeting 7.0.14.254 (WAN-side) and performed LAN and Internet discovery. The flood was active in the digital twin and the attacker remained present.

mitigations.name: Deploy Snort inline IPS between firewall and LAN
mitigations.brief_description: Removed the FortiGate <-> cisco-switch link, added a preconfigured Snort device (snort-ids-1) and created links FortiGate -> snort-ids-1 -> cisco-switch. Snort (preconfigured inline IPS) inspected and blocked the malicious ICMP flood while leaving the compromised host connected.

LAN Connectivity Check (Ping).description: Checks inter-network connectivity by pinging a target host on the LAN. The target IP is defined by the fact 'lan.target.ip'.

Internet Connectivity Check (HTTP).description: Deadman ability that verifies internet connectivity by making an HTTP HEAD request to a well-known public endpoint. Uses curl instead of ping (ICMP) to test TCP-based network reachability. Returns the HTTP status code.

key_observations: The inline Snort placement followed guidance (after the firewall, before internal switch) and immediately reduced the attack success rate from 1.0 to 0.0.
Legitimate LAN (ping 192.168.1.2) and Internet (curl to 1.1.1.1) connectivity were preserved after the change, satisfying live-response constraints.
No compromised host removal/quarantine occurred — the attacker remained present per constraints and mitigation acted at the network layer.
Implementation relied on the environment guarantee that Snort is preconfigured as an IPS; no Snort rule/log details were provided, so effectiveness depends on signature/behavior coverage.
Recommended complementary controls remain valuable: firewall ICMP rate-limiting/anti-DoS, egress filtering, host-level remediation (remove hping3), and enhanced logging/alerting for recurrence.
overall_conclusion: The applied mitigation (adding a preconfigured inline Snort IPS between the FortiGate and LAN) successfully stopped the active ICMP flood while preserving host presence and legitimate connectivity. Follow-up actions should focus on confirming Snort detections, tuning protections, implementing firewall egress/rate-limits, and performing host forensics to remediate the compromised endpoint and prevent future abuse.
